speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Bernice Krantz. Today is February 13, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Ruth Foster. And we're in Winchmore Hill, London, England. And this is tape number 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to apologize. The worst thing which happened to us on the way from [INAUDIBLE] to the ghetto is we were in rows of six, as I said before. The family Grunbech entered all together, my parents, and myself. We walked in a column towards the ghetto, surrounded by SS men-- Latvian SS and German SS-- with rifles. Then, one of the SS men-- in front of us walked a young couple with a little child on their hand, and-- on the father's hand, and the mother had this little girl on her arm. SS man walked up to this little child. I couldn't see whether it was a boy or a girl. We had all lots of clothes on to keep warm. We were told to wear warm clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This SS man asked this little child, do you want a sweetie? And I heard it. My parents, we all heard it. And the child didn't know what to answer. The father said, yes, yes. The SS man said, open your mouth. And with his revolver, he shot through the mouth of the child, and the child was left there, dead. So my father said, I thought we'd come here to work. He whispered to me, what's going on? What's going on? This was on the way to the ghetto. That was our-- Introduction.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was our introduction. And then, we saw what I told you before as we went through the gates in Riga with the puddles of blood and so on. And where did  I leave off now? With the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the vans not having time--

speaker: interviewee
text: The vans that-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to go and come  back.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in February of '42. And it took us a long time before we heard what the fate of those people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they'd been taken to the forests.

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd been taken to the forest. And so had other transports. They say that in the forest of Riga there are 80,000 Jewish souls. In the meantime, we had a relatively proper hospital in Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had supplies coming into--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had supplies to come in from the outside, which was allowed. See, we all-- they formed groups that worked in Riga of the prisoners, of the Jews. Some worked for the SS, some worked for the Wehrmacht-- that means the German army. Some worked for civilian firms. Some worked at the harbors-- you know, import, exports to help, like, dockers that worked, like, dockers. But they lived in the ghetto, came back at night, were marched out in the morning with-- under SS-- being accompanied by SS or Wehrmacht, or German Wehrmacht. It depends which units they were working for. And so those people, under risk of their own life, brought in food in the ghetto. They were exchanging food at work places-- clothing for food, because we found clothes from the poor Latvian Jews. And they took these clothes out into their work places and traded them in, under fear of their own life, with the Latvian civilian people, and smuggled food into the ghetto. And of course, if it was found on you, it was the end of your life. Under risk of life, they smuggled food in. The people-- eventually, it got a little bit more normal. I mean, if you can say it, that it got normal. But people had a little bit more to eat. We had a hospital. And there were doctors-- Latvian Jewish doctors-- working at a pharmaceutical firm in Riga. And under risk of their own lives, they smuggled medicines in. And we had surgical instruments. Women that came with transport that found themselves pregnant still from home-- the pregnancy had to be interrupted. There could not always be abortions, but there had to be proper cesarean operations. But we had a magnificent gynecologist, a German Jew. He had his own clinic in Berlin, a Dr. Josef. And he performed an operation where he [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] didn't give a laparotomy. He didn't cut the mother, but he got the baby out by forceps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even so, the baby wasn't-- wasn't ready to be born. You understand? The mother wasn't left with a scar. It was very skillfully done. And one-- Why-- they didn't want the babies to be born, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not allowed to-- babies to be born. One baby was born. The mother carried the full term of pregnancy. And they called a little boy. And they called this baby Ben Ghetto. The SS-- we had lots of spies in there, as well, you know. We had German Jews. I, unfortunately, must say, they were very in for their own good, you know, with the SS and so on. And it came to our commandant-- it came to his ears that there was a child born in the ghetto. The mother was brought with this little baby of a few days into the hospital, and it had to have his fontanelle-- that's the soft part of the baby's head-- had to be pressed in. He had to be killed. It wasn't shot, but it was killed that way. That was the only child that was born in the ghetto-- Ben Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still working in the hospital when it was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working there, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your parents working? Were they doing any--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. My mother didn't work at that time. She kept house for my uncles and aunts who were working at different units in Riga. My father was working at an SS unit in Riga, and he had to saw wood in the yard. You know, all day long had to saw wood until May-- until the 18th of May, 1942 when he was spotted by a German soldier in this yard where he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Working.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sawing the wood, where he was working. The soldier said, Wilhelm, what are you doing here? So my father said, nothing. He just said, bring me some bread. My father came back in the ghetto at night. He said, I met so-and-so. He told me [INAUDIBLE] name at the time. He is bringing some bread tomorrow, which the soldier did. In the evening, when my father, with his group, came back to the ghetto, there was a gate to the ghetto-- gates. There were inspections by the SS. They found this bread on my father. He had to stand aside. There was a little boy who worked in an SS kitchen. Had to peel potatoes. His father had perished in Salaspils, in this camp where they'd taken all the men at the beginning. So he had taken potato peels so that his mother could cook something from the potato peels. And one German Jew, as well, he was given a sandwich by a German soldier. They found the bread on my father, the potato peels, and the sandwich. Those three-- they were handcuffed. We had Jewish police in the ghetto, like in all ghettos. They came for my mother and me. My mother and I, we had to stand in the front. We all had to assemble on this marketplace-- a [GERMAN], which we called it. My father and this young boy in the middle of 12, and the other men were brought in and handcuffed and put in front of us, and just shot in front of my mother's and my eyes and the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '42. I was 19, 20-- going on for 20. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You could do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't do anything at all. Nothing at all. And I worked still in the hospital. And the conditions were bad, but under those bad conditions, they performed miracles. Most complicated operations. And there were all different recorded aktionen when people were taken away at random. When they would be going, ready to go to their place of work, marching off in their columns to work, the SS would just come in their cars-- they had these open cars-- you know, stopped their cars, jumped out, taken two or three or four or five men out, brought them to the Jewish cemetery for no reason whatsoever, and shot them. And that was [? going ?] on all the time. All the time until-- And then, they made a concentration camp outside Riga, which was called Kaiserwald, where Jewish people worked, and they also had to build the barracks there and so on. That was all what we didn't know in preparation already for the time when the ghetto would be liquidated, which happened on the 2nd of November-- no. In-- we had German Jewish police for the German Jews, for the [GERMAN] ghetto, they called it. And we had Latvian Jewish police for the Latvian Jews across the road, which were fantastic young men. They were all Zionists. They were young students. They were fantastic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they had planned an uprising and the Germans heard about it and they found an arsenal of weapons on the 31st of October, 1942, these 16 young men were marched to the [GERMAN]. And they knew what was up with them. [SOBBING] They were singing the which is the national anthem of Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were singing the and marching to the [GERMAN] where they had to stand up and were-- with machine guns. A machine gun was already there waiting for them with a German SS, and they were shot down. That was the 31st of October, 1942. Things like that happened all the time. On this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, people were dying and starving to death. And-- and I mean, I can't go into details.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your mother managed to get--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother managed to-- she, at that time, worked in the ghetto in a-- oh gosh, I must look dreadful-- in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Let's call it as a dress-maker. They had to repair German uniforms, which came from the Eastern Front. And they had to repair them. There were workshops, then, in the ghetto, and she worked there with other women. My father already had been killed, and I worked as a nurse until the 2nd of November, 1943. 7 o'clock in the morning I had to go to the hospital if I wasn't on night duty. They didn't let any of the groups go to work. The columns were all assembled and they didn't let anybody go to work on the 2nd of November, '43. And I thought, this is very peculiar. But then, eventually, they marched the columns out through the gates. They went to their work places. But the SS was standing there and taking out certain people which couldn't walk very well or elderly or appeared elderly to them. They were put aside. And the rest was allowed to march to their working place. I went to the hospital. And we were three male nurses and 10--

speaker: interviewer
text: Female.

speaker: interviewee
text: --female nurses. And only two of us survived. One is in America, and I'm here. So we were told by the doctor in charge, just get the patients tidy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 13 of you went to the hospital at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to the hospital at 7 o'clock as usual. Get them tidy. Straighten out the beds. Don't wash them, don't take temperature, nothing. Just make them tidy, which we did. And we had no idea what was going to happen. In the meantime, the people went to work again, you know, the columns. My mother was in the ghetto. She worked in the ghetto. Her workshop was in the ghetto itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your hospital was within--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was also within the ghetto walls, or within the ghetto wires. We had the watch towers, the electric wires, and so on. Then, we noticed that lorries came into the ghetto-- open lorries with benches either side, you know, facing each other, and SS men sitting there with rifles. And these lorry went into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we said-- thought to ourselves-- nothing good can come of-- come out of it. So we were not allowed to leave the hospital, but I knew my mother was in the ghetto. I didn't know what would happen to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish police with loud hailers, they're calling the people to assemble at the marketplace, where they were selected by the SS, like this-- left, right, left, right. Left could stay in the ghetto. Right had to [? walk with ?] the lorries. And the lorries were taken out of the ghetto with the people screaming and crying because the rest of the family was at work in the ghettos. They were taken away and didn't know what was going to happen. Later on, [INAUDIBLE] part was taken to Auschwitz and part were taken to the forest. But that day, 10,000 people were taken out of the ghetto and never heard of again. The SS came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was still OK then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The SS came with the lorry to the hospital, and shouted we had to put all the patients onto this lorry. And I will never forget there was one Latvian Jew, a young man, in our hospital who had an injury at work, and he was just there for observation for a day. And he was lying on his knees in front of the commandant. And said, but I can work. He said, I'm able-bodied. I'd only injured my hand. There's nothing much wrong with me. He said, you [GERMAN], and he shot him there as he was kneeling in front of him. And all the time, I was thinking, my mother is still in the ghetto, and the other girls, also. But most of the parents were at work outside the ghetto. My mother was working in the ghetto itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at 5 o'clock in the evening, suddenly it was finished and we were allowed to get out. And I went back to the room where we lived. My mother had a note on the table-- 'The Almighty has rescued me. I was all the time in the base-- in the cellar.' and I'm now [? then ?] there, so I was reunited with her. But that evening of the 2nd of November, 1943, I will never forget as long as I live.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the people came back from their work, the parents had gone, or parents came back and the children had gone. Husbands came bank, the wives and the children had gone. [SOBBING] The wailing and the crying that went on when they realized that everybody had gone. And the ghetto suddenly became a ghost town because 10,000 people had disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would you say were left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 5,000, 6,000. 5,000. And shortly after that, the hospital was liquidated. The whole ghetto was liquidated, and we all came to Kaiserwald, which was a concentration camp of Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was still with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was still with me. And again, they made different units. All the units where the Jews had worked-- they claimed their own Jews. The SS claimed some of the Jews that had worked there as tailors and shoemakers. And they worked there in their workshops. So they had big houses where they had the Jews then. And my mother worked for the Wehrmacht, for the German army, repairing uniforms. So 500 people were taken to work for the German army to repair uniforms. So when I went with my mother. It was called [GERMAN], ABE, [GERMAN]-- the Army Clothing Works. Because the uniforms came from the front, and they had to repair them, and they were sent back to the Eastern Front.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were 500 men and women in this place, which used to be a factory. They called it Ultramarine, directly on the harbor of Riga. That was a warehouse where they [INAUDIBLE] were making blue dye. That's why it was called Ultramarine. It's a color as well, ultramarine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, everything we touched was blue, so we had to clean the place. But we lived there and we worked there. And from then on, I never worked again as a nurse. I went to work in Riga for the [GERMAN] because they had different workshops. And we got the shoes and the boots that had to be repaired and had to be sorted out and so on. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the ABE. The Riga ghetto had already finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living in barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in this Ultramarine. We came out of Kaiserwald. From there, the Germans claimed their Jews-- you understand?-- to work for them. So we worked for the German army, which are not much better than the SS. They say now that army was better, the SS was worse, but they're not much between them. On the 20th of-- we had still few children with us. On the 20th of April or 22nd of April, Hitler's birthday, the last Jewish children were taken away. And they said as a present for Hitler. That was in 1944. On the 28th of July, 1920-- 1944, we went to work as usual, from this Ultramarine where we were housed. Coming back in the evening-- we were different groups, as I said before. We worked for the Germans, but in different units. As the units came back to where we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Staying.

speaker: interviewee
text: In German, they call it [GERMAN]. --where we were [GERMAN], on the yard. We had to-- the yard was surrounded by SS. So we had to undress as we came in. Men and women had to undress completely naked with our little bundle in front of us, and we had to walk in front of these SS people. They had a trestle table. You had to give your name and your age, and walked in front of them. They looked you up and down, and you were sent left or right. You could put your clothes on again. If you were sent to the right, you could go back into your-- into your barracks. If you were sent left, you were taken away straight outside to wait in columns, to wait outside the gates. We came back that evening fairly late. And as I went through this selection, I went into the barracks, went to my mother's bed. You can't call it bed. We were five beds on top of each other. I was right on the fifth, being young and athletic, and my mother was at the bottom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother's bed was not touched, so that means she wasn't there. And I asked-- she worked for the [GERMAN] barracks, infantry barracks. That's where her workshop was. Yes, I had come back already, 4 o'clock in the afternoon, so I knew that she was on the other side-- that she was selected for the other side. Now I can see it-- to the side to die and not to live. If she were to live, she would have been in the barracks. So I crawled down a fire escape, which was outside this barrack. And the whole yard was surrounded by SS with rifle butts. And I still hear until now, where does-- where does the old goat think she is going? And he pulled me down from the fire escape ladder and hit me with his rifle butt over my head and my back. And I've got a nasty injury on my back, which makes me a lot of trouble. Gives me a lot of trouble, especially now I've got arthritis in it. And I'm sometimes in agony.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've still got some traces of-- the yard had, sort of, gravel. And I have gravel in my knee-- here, you can see, it has a scar there-- and in my elbow from the gravel. So they knocked me unconscious, and pulled me back into the barracks. So when I came to, I thought I could do something for my mother, but when I came-- I never saw her again.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to, they had all been taken away. Some people say to Auschwitz, some say to the forest. That, I don't know. But being my mother, being religious, she never had anything [HEBREW], which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: --not kosher. And she left me a note and gave it to one of our Jewish policeman. And said, 'My child, may God bless you.' On Sunday is Tisha B'Av, which is a fast in the Jewish calendar. That is the [INAUDIBLE]. It's the destruction of the second temple. It's a fast day. So maybe, subconsciously, she thought if I should survive, I would know when there's the memorial of her death-- when I would have [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were still living in these older units all over Riga. There were still a few Jews left. And on the 2nd of August, our heads were shaven. We had to assemble, our heads were shaven, and we were given these prison clothes. We had to leave everything behind. No, we were given [INAUDIBLE] We were given grey dresses and little grey scarves for our heads. because they shaved our heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the men were shaved. We called it they gave them the autobahn. You know, Germany is famous for its motorways, the Autobahn. The men were shaven. They shaved a piece out of here and a piece from ear to ear, so that we couldn't escape. The women were shaved completely, and the men just had this autobahn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were marched to the harbor of Riga. Riga is one of the Baltic towns. And there was to ship lying there in the harbor. And we already saw that all-- from all the other people we hadn't seen for years were coming there. All the women shaved, and the men with the autobahn on their head. We were all literally thrown into the bottom of this ship, which usually carries coal. We were as black as-- Coal.

speaker: interviewee
text: --as coal. Without food, without anything. Without luggage. We had to leave the little bits which we acquired in the meantime. We had to leave behind because everything was quick, quick, quick, quick. And you were not allowed to go back to your barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]--
